title: Shock And Aww

jokes.text: Michael was sharing his bed for the first time in years. Unfortunately, it was with his younger brother, Buster.
jokes.setup: The narrator intones that Michael was sharing his bed for the first time in years — romantic or hopeful implication.
jokes.punchline: 'Unfortunately, it was with his younger brother, Buster.' Subverts the expectation of romance with pathetic domesticity.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator misdirection. The setup implies romance, the payoff is Buster — and 'unfortunately' does enormous work in one word. Economy near-perfect. Character integration is strong because Buster being a man-child who ends up in his brother's bed is entirely consistent with who he is. Strong opening joke.

jokes.text: I don't think us sleeping together is working out.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to wake Buster to tell him he has to leave.
jokes.punchline: Using the language of a romantic breakup ('sleeping together is working out') applied to brothers sharing a bed — plays on double meaning.
jokes.explanation: Efficient double-entendre that lands on a slightly uncomfortable note. Wordplay is light but effective. Economy is the strongest component — a lot of comic meaning packed into one sentence. Doesn't quite reach a higher tier because the phrasing is only mildly surprising.

jokes.text: You're a grown man. You should be living with your mother.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to get Buster out of his bed and apartment.
jokes.punchline: 'You should be living with your mother' — in most contexts this is a devastating insult, but for Buster, this is literally the desired outcome and Buster agrees ('Yeah, I miss Mom').
jokes.explanation: Works because Buster's response ('Yeah, I miss Mom') is perfectly in character — what reads as an insult from Michael is aspirational for Buster. Strong character integration. The two-line exchange is very tight.

jokes.text: George Michael asks for help on his ethics essay about whether a preemptive strike is justified; Michael immediately starts dictating confident, glib answers and is annoyed George Michael isn't typing fast enough.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains his ethics homework — is a preemptive strike ever justified?
jokes.punchline: Michael launches into a confident, shallow defense of preemptive strikes and is frustrated George Michael isn't transcribing his rambling. The episode's title is 'Shock and Aww' — direct satirical commentary on the Iraq War.
jokes.explanation: Topical political satire (Iraq War 'Shock and Awe') embedded in a character moment. Michael's blissful confidence in dictating a shallow ethics answer is funny and characterful. Rewatch bonus because the episode title makes it richer in retrospect.

jokes.text: I'm not sure if my ethics teacher would love if I cheated on my essay. / Well, you just passed my ethics test.
jokes.setup: George Michael points out the irony of cheating on an ethics essay.
jokes.punchline: Michael reframes George Michael's scruple as having 'passed his ethics test' — a complete inversion that also reveals Michael's flexible ethical standards, which pay off throughout the episode.
jokes.explanation: Tight, well-structured exchange. Michael's reframe is both funny in the moment and thematically resonant — Michael congratulates his son for being moral while himself doing something immoral (dictating the essay). Rewatch bonus because Michael's own 'ethics' in this episode are demonstrably questionable. Quotable, economical, characterful.

jokes.text: George Michael's secret crush on Ms. Baerly is revealed via a shot of her teaching — 'Even minor crimes are punishable by brutally chopping off the offender's hand' — and George Michael notes 'No ring.'
jokes.setup: Narrator reveals George Michael has a crush on his ethics teacher. We cut to her in class, teaching what sounds like an extreme lesson.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's romantic appraisal is '...No ring.' He's checking if she's available while she's literally describing hand-chopping punishment. The romantic focus is perfectly timed against the violent content.
jokes.explanation: Excellent construction: the violent class content serves as a comic contrast to teenage romantic daydreaming. 'No ring' is perfectly economical and entirely in George Michael's awkward character. Two ideas collide perfectly.

jokes.text: Well, maybe I'll get a son who will... finish his cottage cheese!
jokes.setup: Lucille is complaining about having been signed up to adopt a child without her knowledge. She threatens Michael she might get a 'better' son.
jokes.punchline: The threat deflates catastrophically — the criteria for a better son is simply one who finishes his cottage cheese. Perfectly petty and specific.
jokes.explanation: This is a great Lucille joke — the escalating maternal threat collapses into a trivially petty domestic grievance about cottage cheese. It's deeply character-specific (Lucille's narcissism and control-obsession distilled into food policing), structurally tight, and has a memorably absurd specificity. Justified 8+ score: it's quintessential Lucille Bluth comedy writing.

jokes.text: Yeah, that udder's been dry for a while now, hasn't it?
jokes.setup: Lucille says she doesn't have 'the milk of mother's kindness' in her anymore.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately extends the metaphor: 'that udder's been dry for a while now' — reducing his mother to livestock and suggesting her maternal warmth has been absent for years.
jokes.explanation: Efficient wordplay building on Lucille's own metaphor. The son calling his mother a 'dry udder' is darkly funny and character-specific. Slightly below the cottage cheese line in impact but still a solid punch.

jokes.text: Gob calls Michael offering 'a couple of fun girls' as an olive branch after the Marta situation.
jokes.setup: Narrator notes Gob discovered Michael was interested in his ex-girlfriend Marta. Gob frames his call as offering an 'olive branch.'
jokes.punchline: The 'olive branch' is Gob procuring women for Michael — his version of reconciliation, completely tone-deaf to actual conflict resolution.
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Gob's idea of making amends is setting up a double date, which tells us everything about him. The callback to the Marta situation gives it context. Not a standout moment but competent.

jokes.text: Besides, when was the last time you slept with someone? / Buster. / What time and where?
jokes.setup: Gob asks Michael when he last slept with someone, intending it as a rhetorical dig.
jokes.punchline: Michael answers 'Buster' — referring to sharing a bed — and Buster, overhearing, misinterprets it as an invitation and asks 'What time and where?' — a triple-layered misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes and earns the high score. Three-way comedic collision: Gob's rhetorical taunt, Michael's literal-but-awkward answer (Buster, from the opening scene), and Buster's oblivious eagerness in 'What time and where?' — treating it as a genuine romantic proposal. Each character's response is perfectly in-character. The structure is elegant: one short exchange, three distinct comic beats, a callback to the opening, and Buster's line lands as the perfect cap. Quotable, rewatch-valuable, and unmistakably character-specific.

jokes.text: Lindsay mistakes George Michael's crush on Ms. Baerly as wanting to set up his father, believing George Michael wants someone to 'fill the role of his mother.'
jokes.setup: George Michael tells Lindsay he likes Ms. Baerly. Lindsay interprets this correctly — mostly — and concludes George Michael wants a mother figure for Michael.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Lindsay's interpretation is completely self-serving — she missed the actual point (George Michael is in love with the teacher himself).
jokes.explanation: Works as a plot-engine joke — Lindsay's misreading drives much of the episode's comedy of errors. Not a big laugh-out-loud moment but competently constructed irony.

jokes.text: You're my aunt. / That doesn't matter. Aunts can fill that role. Teachers can fill that role. And someday you're gonna find the right woman to fill that role. But until then, I'll be right across the hall.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is explaining that a woman can fill the 'mother role' in George Michael's life. George Michael protests that she is his aunt.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay dismisses the objection entirely and — completely obliviously — offers herself as a romantic/maternal substitute for her teenage nephew, ending with the ominous 'I'll be right across the hall.' The narrator then adds: 'Lindsay had never been more proud of anything she had said in her entire life.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best. Lindsay's oblivious inappropriateness escalates with each sentence — she is arguing herself into a deeply uncomfortable position without any self-awareness. The payoff of 'I'll be right across the hall' is perfectly ominous and the narrator's kicker — 'Lindsay had never been more proud' — seals it as a masterclass in character-based cringe comedy. Also seeds ongoing Maeby/George Michael 'cousin' tension in the show's DNA. Justified 8+.

jokes.text: Lindsay had never been more proud of anything she had said in her entire life.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just delivered an unwittingly inappropriate speech about filling her nephew's mother-role.
jokes.punchline: The narrator declares it the proudest moment of her life — treating her oblivious blunder as a personal achievement.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's use as a comedy instrument is one of AD's signature techniques, and this is a clean example. Economy is exceptional — it adds maximum devastation with minimal words. It amplifies the preceding joke rather than being independent of it, working as a cap. Counted separately because it's a distinct comedic beat (the narrator's voice is the punchline).

jokes.text: This is Shannon, the girl I've been telling you all about. / Yes. Girl, indeed... young girl. That's a young, young, young, girl. / Yeah, well, she's 18.
jokes.setup: Gob has set Michael up on a double date, claiming he's offering an 'olive branch.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's horrified reaction to Shannon's apparent youth is delivered as increasingly panicked reassurances to himself ('young, young, young girl'), while Gob's response ('she's 18') only barely clears the bar and doesn't reassure anyone.
jokes.explanation: Michael's repeated 'young, young, young girl' is a nice character-comedy beat — his alarm is both genuine and performed. Gob's '18' defense is perfectly insufficient. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Shannon wouldn't go out without a chaperone, and so she's your date. (Nazhgalia, an older woman with a mustache, is Michael's blind date)
jokes.setup: Gob claimed he was offering Michael an olive branch with a fun double date.
jokes.punchline: Michael's actual date is Nazhgalia — ostensibly a chaperone, clearly Gob's revenge. She has a visible mustache, which Michael nearly points out before catching himself.
jokes.explanation: The olive branch revealed as sabotage is a clean structural reversal. The visual of Nazhgalia (and the mustache near-slip) is the primary laugh. Competent but relies on a visual we're partly inferring from dialogue.

jokes.text: Oh... 'Nagarma,' you've got a mustache. / Hey! / I mean, you've got milk on your mustache. / Oh. / I mean, you've got a milk mustache. / It's adorable. You look like one of those 'Got Milk' models.
jokes.setup: Michael accidentally begins to comment on Nazhgalia's mustache.
jokes.punchline: Michael mid-sentence tries to course-correct twice — 'milk on your mustache' → 'milk mustache' — before landing on the absurd compliment of comparing her to a 'Got Milk' ad model. Each recovery makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating cringe. Each attempted recovery from Michael is worse than the last, culminating in an absurd compliment that still centers the mustache. This is the kind of comedic escalation AD excels at — Michael's social decency turned into a trap. The three-step structure (mustache → milk on mustache → milk mustache → Got Milk) is airtight. Earns its near-8 score.

jokes.text: Gob, jealous of Michael's basic human decency toward Nazhgalia, suddenly declares her 'exotic' and 'very attractive' to compete.
jokes.setup: Michael manages to find something kind to say to Nazhgalia (the 'Got Milk' line). Narrator notes Gob 'mistook Michael's basic human decency for romantic interest.'
jokes.punchline: Gob, competitively, pivots to hitting on Michael's date — entirely because he can't stand Michael succeeding at anything, even an unwanted blind date.
jokes.explanation: Gob's irrational sibling rivalry is perfectly consistent character comedy. The narrator's framing ('basic human decency for romantic interest') is where the real craft is — Gob is so competitive he can't distinguish basic kindness from flirting.

jokes.text: I've also lost four pounds on your low-carb Bluth Banana Jail Bars.
jokes.setup: Cindi is describing her devotion to George Sr.'s teachings.
jokes.punchline: 'Bluth Banana Jail Bars' — the Bluth family banana stand has been extended into a prison diet product. The specificity of 'four pounds' on a jailhouse low-carb banana bar is perfectly absurd.
jokes.explanation: The Bluth banana brand extended into prison diet bars is a wonderfully absurd escalation of a running gag. 'Jail Bars' is a tidy wordplay on the prison context. Character-specific to the Bluth family's opportunistic grift. Earns a solid score.

jokes.text: I've come to learn at your feet. / That's a good place to start.
jokes.setup: Cindi says she's come to 'learn at his feet' — a spiritual/metaphorical statement of discipleship.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s response 'That's a good place to start' reframes the spiritual language as physically literal (and sexually suggestive), delivered as though it's wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Clean double entendre delivered with precision. George Sr.'s corrupt spirituality is perfectly captured — he hears 'learn at your feet' as an opportunity. Economy is excellent. The pause implied before 'That's a good place to start' is everything.

jokes.text: Hey, Linds, is it my imagination or does this rape room have the same floor plan as our kitchen?
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at Parent-Teacher Night and begins observing the school building.
jokes.punchline: He casually refers to a room as a 'rape room' while making a mundane observation about floor plans — treating the outrageous label as completely unremarkable.
jokes.explanation: A great non-sequitur joke. The shock of 'rape room' is maximized by the casualness of the floor plan observation — Michael is making a home-decor complaint, not a moral one. The banality around an extreme word is the joke's engine. Earns its score through originality and economy, though the earned-vs-cheap score is slightly docked because 'rape room' is a strong word used for shock value.

jokes.text: Mmm. No ring. (Michael spots Ms. Baerly for the first time at Parent-Teacher Night)
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly introduces herself at Parent-Teacher Night.
jokes.punchline: Michael's thought/reaction is 'No ring' — the exact same line George Michael used when first spotting her. Father and son have the same first instinct about the same woman.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes for structural elegance. Two words. No additional framing needed. The callback to George Michael's identical line illuminates the entire father-son dynamic — their shared loneliness, their identical romantic instincts — without a single word of exposition. Maximum economy, perfect callback structure, deeply character-integrated. Justified 8+ score.

jokes.text: Ethics... right and wrong. How can one thing can be right and another one be wrong? I mean, which is which, you know? I guess that's the 'Rrrr' of it.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to flirt with Ms. Baerly by engaging with ethics as a subject.
jokes.punchline: 'I guess that's the Rrrr of it' — Michael's philosophical engagement with ethics collapses into a frustrated growl, revealing he has absolutely nothing to say on the subject.
jokes.explanation: 'The Rrrr of it' is a lovely piece of character comedy — Michael is smart enough to know he should engage but not smart enough to actually do it. The verbal tic becoming a shared joke with Beth later is a nice running gag seed.

jokes.text: I had him, Mr. Daniels. How is he? / Oh, he, you know, had a stroke. / Thought you were joking. / No, I was just laughing 'cause you were laughing. / Right. No. Yeah. Gosh.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly mentions she got the ethics class after Mr. Daniels had a stroke. Michael asks after him lightheartedly.
jokes.punchline: Both of them have been laughing at the stroke — neither knows why — and the spiral of awkward recognition that they've been treating a stroke as a joke.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully observed cringe moment — both characters fall into social-laughter contagion, then have to reckon with what they were laughing at. The dialogue spiral ('Thought you were joking / No, I was just laughing because you were laughing') is naturalistic and funny. Good craft.

jokes.text: Can a parent take a teacher out for a drink? Or... I don't know. Uh, do you have a wife? / Yeah, but she's dead.
jokes.setup: Michael is clumsily asking Ms. Baerly out, and asks if she has a wife (clearly a gender-swapped version of asking if he has a wife).
jokes.punchline: Ms. Baerly confirms she had a husband but he's dead — a turn from the awkward flirting into genuine tragedy, which then gets looped back into the cringe spiral.
jokes.explanation: Decent setup for the 'wife died' running gag in this scene. Michael's clumsy approach to asking about relationship status reveals both his social awkwardness and the genuine tragedy of his life (his wife also died).

jokes.text: Have you ever been married? / Yeah, she died. / Ohh. God, why are we laughing? / I don't... Well, it's the Mr. Daniels stroke thing all over again.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly asks Michael if he's ever been married. He confirms his wife died.
jokes.punchline: They're both laughing again — immediately recognizing this is a repeat of the Mr. Daniels stroke situation — laughing at something deeply sad, unable to stop.
jokes.explanation: The running gag of 'we're laughing at tragic things' pays off beautifully here. Michael's dead wife is an established emotional weight in the show, and having them both laugh-spiral about it parallels the Daniels stroke beat almost immediately. The self-awareness ('it's the Mr. Daniels stroke thing all over again') is perfectly timed and characterizes both people as fundamentally compatible in their inappropriate social responses. Strong callback structure.

jokes.text: George Sr. asks Cindi to confess sins, specifically steering her back to 'the ladies' shower in your college dorm again.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. suggests Cindi could tell him some of his sins, but quickly acknowledges legal implications and steers back to hers.
jokes.punchline: 'Back to your sins. Uh, do that one about the, uh... ladies' shower in your college dorm again.' The 'again' is the kicker — this is a repeat performance. George Sr. is using his spiritual authority as a voyeuristic confessional service.
jokes.explanation: The word 'again' is doing all the heavy lifting — suggesting this is a curated, repeated sin-recounting performance for George Sr.'s benefit. His spiritual fraud made viscerally corrupt in one word. Strong character integration — this is exactly who George Sr. is.

jokes.text: Michael, the little Korean is here, and I don't know what to do with him. At least, I think it's a him. You've got to strip them down to next to nothing before you can even tell.
jokes.setup: Lucille calls Michael to report that the adopted Korean child has arrived.
jokes.punchline: Her description of the child is comically dehumanizing and clueless — 'at least, I think it's a him' and 'strip them down to next to nothing' treat a child like an unidentified package.
jokes.explanation: Deeply Lucille — her racism and narcissism combine to produce complete dehumanization of a child she didn't want. 'Strip them down to next to nothing before you can even tell' is darkly funny and perfectly in character. High character integration score is warranted.

jokes.text: Yeah, Mom. I just spoke to Social Services and although they don't like to do this, if you can prove that it's a bad environment for a child — and I would suggest saying what you just said to me, don't change a word — they will take him back.
jokes.setup: Michael is advising Lucille on how to get rid of the adopted child.
jokes.punchline: 'I would suggest saying what you just said to me, don't change a word' — Michael is recommending Lucille repeat her xenophobic, dehumanizing description of the child as evidence of her unfitness. Implies it's already damning enough.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured callback joke — Michael's legal advice is 'please repeat your crimes.' The callback to the preceding dehumanizing speech is crisp. 'Don't change a word' is an excellent punchline cap. The fact that Michael is both horrified and pragmatic captures his character perfectly.

jokes.text: Lucille to the adopted child: 'Ha, ha, ha. Uh, don't get too comfortable. Shoes on, mister! Shoes on! He's out of control.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is trying to establish dominance over the young child she just received.
jokes.punchline: 'He's out of control' about a child who has presumably just removed shoes — Lucille's dramatic overreaction to minor child behavior.
jokes.explanation: Lucille declaring a shoe-removing child 'out of control' is funny character work — her expectations of rigid control and obedience applied to a child who just arrived. Works as a quick beat.

jokes.text: What, I'm gone for a couple days and you find a new son?
jokes.setup: Buster discovers Lucille has adopted Annyong.
jokes.punchline: Buster expresses jealousy over being replaced — a fully grown adult is jealous of a child usurping his maternal spot.
jokes.explanation: Buster's infantilized relationship with Lucille is perfectly expressed in this one line. 'A couple days' — he wasn't even gone long. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Lucille could see that her son was concerned, even jealous. And she knew how it felt to be overlooked.
jokes.setup: Buster is jealous of Annyong getting Lucille's attention.
jokes.punchline: The narrator implies Lucille empathizes with being overlooked — the joke being that Lucille never actually behaves with empathy and is herself the cause of most family members feeling overlooked.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan attribution of empathy to Lucille is mildly ironic but not particularly sharp. Functional setup for what follows rather than a standalone joke. Middle-of-the-road.

jokes.text: Yes. Annyong is your brother now. / Yes, Annyong.
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Buster that Annyong is his brother. Annyong says 'Annyong' (which means 'hello' in Korean).
jokes.punchline: Every time Annyong speaks, he says 'Annyong' — which the family interprets as his name. He keeps 'confirming' his name while actually just saying hello. This is the introduction of a running gag.
jokes.explanation: The Annyong/'hello' gag is a signature AD running joke. The layered misunderstanding — the family thinks 'Annyong' is a name when it's a greeting — works on multiple levels and gets richer with each repetition. Rewatch bonus because knowing the joke makes every earlier instance funnier.

jokes.text: Yes, Annyong. Excuse me. Oh, Michael? Call it off. / I'm keeping him.
jokes.setup: Michael has called Social Services to take Annyong back. Lucille initially seems to want him gone.
jokes.punchline: After a moment with Annyong (presumably the boy says 'Annyong'/hello again), Lucille reverses completely: 'I'm keeping him.' The implied beat — Annyong simply existing — has charmed her.
jokes.explanation: The reversal is funny because we never see what wins Lucille over — just the result. Implies Annyong said 'Annyong' (hello) and she misread it as affection. Clean, efficiently constructed.

jokes.text: No, no. That was my Mom. She just had a 'little Korean' dropped off. / Ooh, that sounds good. Let's have that.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Ms. Baerly that his mom 'had a little Korean dropped off.'
jokes.punchline: Ms. Baerly mishears/misunderstands — interpreting 'a little Korean' as a food order and eagerly asking to have that for dinner.
jokes.explanation: Sharp misunderstanding joke with an excellent punchline. 'That sounds good, let's have that' is perfectly timed and delivered — Ms. Baerly's excitement about Korean food collides with the actual meaning. Economy is excellent. A well-constructed single-exchange joke.

jokes.text: Michael sneaks Ms. Baerly into the model home at night, and they are discovered by Buster and Gob — who independently are also sneaking in.
jokes.setup: Michael and Ms. Baerly are sneaking upstairs to his room. He assures her they'll be quiet.
jokes.punchline: The staircase becomes a traffic jam of siblings — Buster and Gob are both sneaking in, turning a romantic moment into a family reunion on the stairs.
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy sequence inferred from dialogue. The escalation of 'I'm sorry, Michael' from multiple brothers is a nice visual gag. The irony of trying to sneak a date in while siblings are also sneaking around is very AD.

jokes.text: I'm sorry too, Michael, but it looks like we're even. You took something I liked. I took something you liked.
jokes.setup: Gob is confronted on the staircase after sleeping with Nazhgalia (Michael's blind date, whom Michael didn't want).
jokes.punchline: Gob considers sleeping with Nazhgalia revenge — 'you took something I liked, I took something you liked' — despite Michael never having wanted Nazhgalia in the first place.
jokes.explanation: Gob's skewed logic — treating an unwanted blind date as a valued possession to be stolen — is perfectly in character. The joke's engine is Gob's complete misunderstanding of what constitutes revenge. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Listen, I know that you've been trying to get even with me about Marta. But got bad news for ya. I'm seeing somebody else. / Who? Who? / Just out of curiosity — I'm not gonna try to sleep with her.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob he's seeing someone new and Gob will not sleep with her.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately asks 'Who?' twice — so instinctively competitive he can't help himself — then immediately qualifies 'just out of curiosity, I'm not gonna try to sleep with her.' His instinct is stronger than his self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: Gob's 'Who? Who?' is the joke — it's purely Pavlovian. The disclaimer that follows is funnier for being immediately contradicted. Strong character integration; this is unmistakably Gob.

jokes.text: Forget it. I thought this was behind us. / It is behind us. / Great. / When I sleep with her. 'Cause I'm gonna sleep with her.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to close the Marta conversation. Gob agrees 'it is behind us.'
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately qualifies: 'When I sleep with her. 'Cause I'm gonna sleep with her.' The agreement was entirely conditional.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best line-for-line exchanges. The structure is perfect: false agreement → complete reversal. 'It is behind us. When I sleep with her. 'Cause I'm gonna sleep with her.' Three sentences that escalate and subvert simultaneously. Gob's character is entirely contained in the logic. Earns its 8+ through structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Yeah, I was lying when I said I wasn't gonna sleep with her, so...
jokes.setup: Continuing the confrontation about Gob's intentions.
jokes.punchline: Gob simply admits he lied — the disclaimer was itself a lie. The casual 'so...' trailing off is the finishing touch.
jokes.explanation: The admission is disarming in its directness. Gob's complete lack of shame about the lie is the engine. The trailing 'so...' implies he considers this self-explanatory. High economy and character integration.

jokes.text: She'd have to agree to it. / I don't anticipate a problem with that. / No? / No. 'Cause I've seen some of the dogs you like. / Right. / I just slept with one.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Gob would need his new woman's consent. Gob declares he doesn't anticipate a problem.
jokes.punchline: 'Cause I've seen some of the dogs you like. / I just slept with one.' Gob insults Michael's taste in women while simultaneously revealing he slept with Nazhgalia — whose appearance was already established as the joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'dogs you like' punchline lands as both an insult and a self-own — Gob has just admitted to sleeping with a woman he described as a 'dog.' The logic ties together cleanly.

jokes.text: George Michael has been up making a poster of Saddam Hussein in a bathing suit for Ms. Baerly because 'she loves Saddam Hussein.'
jokes.setup: George Michael reveals he's made a gift for his ethics teacher — a poster of Saddam Hussein.
jokes.punchline: Michael gently corrects: 'I'm sure she doesn't love Saddam. I'm sure she is interested in him as a subject.' George Michael: 'Right. That's what I meant.' The poster is of Saddam in a bathing suit.
jokes.explanation: The Saddam Hussein bathing suit poster is a wonderfully specific absurd detail — teenage romantic logic filtered through political subject matter. George Michael's earnest literalism ('she loves Saddam') is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: You mean you love her like... like she loves Saddam? / No, no. I mean, like love her love her.
jokes.setup: Michael realizes George Michael has a genuine romantic crush on Ms. Baerly.
jokes.punchline: Michael tries to soften the realization by relating it to the Saddam analogy — and George Michael corrects him with 'love her love her,' the distinction being both emotionally earnest and funny.
jokes.explanation: Using the Saddam love-interest as a foil for genuine romantic love is an unexpected and funny structural move. 'Love her love her' is endearingly earnest and the contrast with the absurdity of the Saddam context gives it weight. Strong callback integration.

jokes.text: Michael tells George Michael that Gob slept with Ms. Baerly — when actually Michael was the one dating her.
jokes.setup: Michael knows George Michael likes Ms. Baerly and that he (Michael) has been dating her — and George Michael just saw her leaving the house.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'ethical responsibility' leads him to tell George Michael 'Your Uncle Gob slept with her' — a complete lie that protects Michael while framing Gob. The narrator drily notes: 'Michael knew he had an ethical responsibility to tell his son the truth.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes. The narrator setup ('Michael knew he had an ethical responsibility to tell his son the truth') directly precedes Michael lying. The thematic irony — the man who gave his son an 'ethics test' at breakfast fails his own — is beautifully constructed. This is earned comedy: the setup was laid at the opening of the episode and pays off here. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: Michael knew he had an ethical responsibility to tell his son the truth. / Yes. / Your Uncle Gob slept with her.
jokes.setup: Narrator sets up the expectation that Michael will do the right thing: 'Michael knew he had an ethical responsibility to tell his son the truth.'
jokes.punchline: Michael confirms — 'Yes' — and then tells the lie: 'Your Uncle Gob slept with her.' The narrator's framing of this as fulfilling an ethical responsibility makes the lie funnier.
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-structural-irony at its finest. Setting up 'Michael knew he had an ethical responsibility to tell the truth' and then having him lie — in an episode ABOUT ethics, where he was romancing the ETHICS teacher — is layered and perfect. The narrator's deadpan builds the expectation; Michael's 'Yes' appears to confirm it; 'Your Uncle Gob slept with her' demolishes it. The thematic resonance with the episode's ethics motif makes this exceptional. One of the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Gob, he just... He's not that exact of a target shooter, and he just kind of sprays it everywhere.
jokes.setup: George Michael can't understand why Gob would sleep with his ethics teacher just to get even with Michael.
jokes.punchline: Michael's explanation: Gob is 'not that exact of a target shooter' and 'sprays it everywhere' — a firearms metaphor for Gob's indiscriminate sexual revenge, delivered as an explanation to his teenage son.
jokes.explanation: The euphemism is clever and the image it creates is very funny — Gob as a man who 'sprays it everywhere.' Appropriate that Michael deploys this metaphor to explain Gob to his teenage son, which adds an awkward parenting layer.

jokes.text: Don't you think you should be taking somebody your own age? / Like your cousin. / That's a bad example, but...
jokes.setup: Michael suggests George Michael should date someone his own age.
jokes.punchline: Michael's example is George Michael's cousin — which he immediately walks back as 'a bad example.' The Maeby/George Michael cousin romantic tension is a season-long running gag, and Michael himself almost suggesting it is a perfect dark joke.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. Michael's instant self-correction ('That's a bad example, but...') implies he almost meant it — which is funnier than if he'd never said it. Rewatch bonus is high because this pays off constantly across the series. For AD fans this is a genuine laugh-out-loud moment of character-based dark comedy.

jokes.text: I defaced a rare book to get that picture of Saddam Hussein in a bathing suit. / 'What would Saddam do?' / Yeah.
jokes.setup: George Michael is processing his embarrassment over the Ms. Baerly situation.
jokes.punchline: He reveals he defaced a rare book to obtain the Saddam Hussein bathing suit photo. Michael's response: 'What would Saddam do?' — sardonically suggesting Saddam would probably retaliate — which George Michael takes as genuine validation.
jokes.explanation: The commitment to the Saddam Hussein joke pays off magnificently here. George Michael defacing a rare book to get a bathing suit photo of Saddam as a romantic gesture is absurdist perfection. 'What would Saddam do?' as a WWJD parody that Michael delivers sardonically but George Michael takes literally is pitch-perfect. Highly quotable, original, and earns its score.

jokes.text: Okay, that is our exact outdoor fire pit. (Lindsay recognizing the model home's decor at school)
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Michael have been noting the similarities between the model home and the school's layout throughout the Parent-Teacher Night scene.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay spots yet another identical home element — the outdoor fire pit — in the school.
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag callback but not particularly sharp on its own. The cumulative observation that the school was built from Bluth model home plans is funny as a concept but this particular line doesn't add much. Middle tier.

jokes.text: You know, I actually called Mr. Daniels and asked him (to go to the diversity dance), but he got all out of breath and dropped the phone. I never heard back.
characters: Tobias (or Maeby — context suggests this is likely Maeby based on the school setting)
jokes.setup: The family expert on 'making trouble' (appears to be Maeby) is advising George Michael on taking a shocking date to the diversity dance.
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals she called Mr. Daniels — the stroke victim from earlier — inviting him to a high school dance, he 'got all out of breath and dropped the phone,' and she simply 'never heard back' without concern.
jokes.explanation: An outstanding callback to the Mr. Daniels stroke bit. The revelation that someone called a stroke patient to invite him to a diversity dance — and he 'dropped the phone' mid-call and was never followed up on — is dark comedy at AD's finest. 'I never heard back' as a dismissal of what is possibly a medical emergency is perfectly deadpan. The economy is excellent and the callback structure is tight. Earns its 8+ easily.

jokes.text: I know he was dating that girl Shannon. The cheerleader? / Yeah, she's probably gonna take him to that stupid diversity dance. I wish I had someone shocking to take.
jokes.setup: George Michael wants to get revenge on Gob by taking someone shocking to the diversity dance.
jokes.punchline: The irony that he's talking to Maeby — his cousin — about wanting someone 'shocking,' while she's right there as the most shocking option.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is clear but not labored. George Michael wanting 'someone shocking' while speaking to his cousin-crush is a nice layered moment for rewatchers.

jokes.text: You know, maybe we should go together. / Right, I mean, it's a bad example... I... But should we?
jokes.setup: Maeby suggests she and George Michael go to the diversity dance together.
jokes.punchline: George Michael echoes Michael's 'bad example' phrasing from earlier — implicitly acknowledging the cousin problem — before asking 'But should we?' His logic collapses into the same mistake Michael almost made.
jokes.explanation: George Michael using Michael's exact phrasing ('that's a bad example') while himself making the same error is a beautiful piece of structural comedy. The 'But should we?' following the self-correction is a wonderful moment of the show leaning into its own darkest running gag. Rewatch bonus for how this echoes across the season.

jokes.text: Yes, Annyong. Your name's Annyong. We all know you're Annyong. Annyong. Annyong. Annyong.
jokes.setup: Annyong keeps saying 'Annyong' (hello in Korean). The family thinks it's his name.
jokes.punchline: Lucille exasperatedly uses his 'name' repeatedly, not realizing he's just greeting her repeatedly. The repetition of 'Annyong' becomes its own rhythmic joke.
jokes.explanation: The Annyong gag continues to build. Lucille's exasperated repetition of what she thinks is the child's name — while he just keeps saying hello — is a well-sustained running joke. Lucille's voice makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Annyong keeps saying 'Annyong' (which means 'hello' in Korean) and everyone thinks it's his name. Lucille: 'Yes, Annyong. Your name's Annyong. We all know you're Annyong. Annyong. Annyong. Annyong.'
jokes.setup: The family has named the adopted Korean child 'Annyong' — which is actually just him saying 'hello' in Korean.
jokes.punchline: Lucille increasingly frustrated repetition of 'Annyong' — essentially shouting 'hello' at the child repeatedly — while thinking she's asserting his name.
jokes.explanation: The 'Annyong = hello' joke is a classic AD planted gag — the audience learns the double meaning through context and Lucille's oblivious repetition. Her frustrated 'We ALL know you're Annyong. Annyong. Annyong.' is increasingly absurd as she's just repeatedly saying 'hello hello hello' at a confused child. The joke will pay off repeatedly across the series. Justified 8+ for originality, character integration, and the long-game payoff.

jokes.text: So we're related. / Hey, wanna go to a dance? / Oh, great, another uncle to compete with.
jokes.setup: George Michael introduces Annyong as his new little brother. Maeby observes they're now related.
jokes.punchline: Annyong says 'Annyong' (hello), which prompts someone to interpret it as accepting a dance invitation. Then Maeby's aside: 'Oh, great, another uncle to compete with' — implying she is romantically competing for George Michael's attention against uncles.
jokes.explanation: Dense layering: the Annyong hello-as-name joke, the cousin romantic tension, and Maeby's 'another uncle' line which implies a competitive romantic dynamic with actual uncles (deeply wrong and very funny). AD at its most efficiently layered.

jokes.text: Ms. Baerly, teaching about atrocities, says 'I'm sorry. I met someone. I'm sorry.' then catches herself: 'Anyway, this is serious. This is atrocities. No more smiling.'
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly is teaching a class about political prisoners and atrocities — serious subject matter.
jokes.punchline: She breaks mid-lesson to process her own romantic feelings ('I'm sorry. I met someone.') before trying to snap back to professionalism. The private emotional bleed-through into a lesson on atrocities is perfectly timed.
jokes.explanation: 'No more smiling. This is atrocities.' is a perfect line — the inappropriate happiness juxtaposed against the lesson content, and her attempt to self-correct while clearly still happy. Strong character work for a minor character.

jokes.text: Don't worry. George Michael's not in there. Those are the dumb kids.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at Ms. Baerly's classroom. She notices him looking toward the classroom door.
jokes.punchline: She reassures him that George Michael isn't in that class — then adds 'those are the dumb kids' as if that's an appropriate clarifying statement for a teacher to make.
jokes.explanation: Economy is the star here — 'those are the dumb kids' is such a casual violation of teacher ethics that it generates a laugh in under five words. Fits Ms. Baerly's slightly inappropriate, unguarded quality.

jokes.text: Wow! Did you make this for me? This is so sweet. I love Hussein.
jokes.setup: Michael brings George Michael's Saddam Hussein poster to Beth as context for breaking up.
jokes.punchline: Beth, seeing the Saddam poster, says 'I love Hussein' — using the exact phrasing Michael earlier corrected George Michael for, confirming the correction was wrong.
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff — Michael told his son 'she doesn't love Saddam, she's interested in him as a subject,' and Beth immediately says 'I love Hussein.' Michael was wrong to correct George Michael, which retroactively makes his correction funnier. Clean structural callback.

jokes.text: Where did you find this one of him in a Speedo?
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly sees the poster George Michael made — Saddam Hussein in a bathing suit.
jokes.punchline: Rather than being horrified or confused, she asks where Michael found the Speedo photo — implying she's impressed and curious about the sourcing of a dictator's swimwear photo.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Speedo' is the joke — it escalates from 'bathing suit' to something more precise and absurd. Ms. Baerly's appreciative curiosity (rather than alarm) about finding a Saddam Hussein swimwear photo is perfect character comedy. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: You really mean it? This isn't one of 'my wife died' jokes?
jokes.setup: Michael tells Ms. Baerly he can't continue seeing her. She asks if he's serious.
jokes.punchline: 'This isn't one of my wife died jokes?' — she has categorized Michael's dead-wife disclosure as a genre of joke he does, suggesting it's happened enough to become a recognizable pattern.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'My wife died jokes' as a genre is inspired — the implication that Michael has deployed this enough that it's now a recognizable comedic category for Beth is both dark and brilliantly constructed. The economy is exceptional. High quotability. Strong callback payoff to the laughing-at-tragedy running thread.

jokes.text: I told him that you slept with my brother. / That may be the most unethical thing I've ever heard. / Well, you've only been doing this half a semester.
jokes.setup: Michael admits he told his son that she slept with his brother (a lie). Ms. Baerly is appalled.
jokes.punchline: 'Well, you've only been doing this half a semester' — Michael diminishes her ethics expertise by pointing out she's a novice, thereby defending his lie with meta-commentary on her credentials.
jokes.explanation: This is arguably the episode's best joke. Michael has just done something deeply unethical — lying to his son about who slept with his teacher — and when the ethics teacher calls it 'the most unethical thing she's ever heard,' he counters by undercutting her credentials. The perfect irony: the man who gave his son an 'ethics test' in the opening scene is now debating his ethics with someone who's 'only been doing this half a semester.' Structure is near-perfect. Extremely quotable. Earns its top score.

jokes.text: Look, it was a preemptive strike. My brother would've tried to sleep with you.
jokes.setup: Michael defends lying to his son about who slept with Ms. Baerly.
jokes.punchline: He calls it a 'preemptive strike' — the exact term from George Michael's ethics essay at the start of the episode. Michael is now applying the ethics essay concept (which he confidently explained) to justify his own unethical behavior.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural crown jewel. Michael's use of 'preemptive strike' — the exact ethical question from George Michael's homework that Michael answered so confidently and glibly at the start — to justify his own profoundly unethical behavior is thematically perfect, structurally elegant, and deeply character-integrated. This is what top-tier comedy writing looks like: the episode's premise (ethics, preemptive strikes) paid off in a character moment that is both funny and revealing. The episode title 'Shock and Aww' (the Iraq War 'Shock and Awe' doctrine, which is preemptive force) makes this even richer on rewatch. This is the episode's best joke.

jokes.text: And you will not see me again. / Starting right now. (Michael lingers awkwardly at the door)
jokes.setup: Michael declares he will not be seen again, promises to leave.
jokes.punchline: 'Starting right now' — he does not immediately leave. The declaration and the action are separated by an uncomfortable beat that undermines the dramatic exit.
jokes.explanation: 'Starting right now' as a qualifier that undermines the declaration is a clean comedic beat. Michael's inability to execute a clean exit is character-consistent. Economical and effective.

jokes.text: George Sr. is pinching the hidden surveillance camera — 'He's pinching the camera. He's on to us.' / 'Get the dog. Clean the dog. We are cleaning the dog!'
jokes.setup: S.E.C. agents have hidden a camera on a dog to surveil George Sr. during his walk with Cindi.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. notices the camera. The agent team escalates into panic — 'Get the dog. Clean the dog. We are cleaning the dog!' — an absurd surveillance operation falling apart via dog-grooming.
jokes.explanation: 'We are cleaning the dog!' as a covert ops abort command is absurdly funny — the professionalism of surveillance colliding with the absurdity of having used a dog as a camera housing. The escalation from calm monitoring to 'clean the dog' is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Watch your back, my little immigrant friend! (Lucille to Annyong from a moving car)
jokes.setup: Lucille is giving Maeby and Annyong a ride, and appears to be lecturing Annyong about something.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's parting words to the child she just adopted: 'Watch your back, my little immigrant friend!' — simultaneously possessive (he's hers now) and threatening.
jokes.explanation: Deeply Lucille. The mixture of affection and menace, the casual racism, and the formal 'my little immigrant friend' as a term of address for a child she just adopted is exactly the kind of dark specificity that makes Lucille one of TV's great comic characters.

jokes.text: So you're not letting him in because he doesn't share your perfectly shaped nose, your round eye-shaped eyes, your strong square jaw. / Thanks. You wanna dance? / Yeah! All right! Steve Holt!
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to get into the diversity dance by arguing against discrimination. He compliments the doorman's features extravagantly.
jokes.punchline: The doorman takes the compliments sincerely, immediately invites Gob to dance, and Gob enthusiastically accepts — completely forgetting he was trying to get past him. 'Steve Holt!' is delivered with Gob's signature over-the-top energy.
jokes.explanation: An unexpected reversal — the compliment gambit works too well. Gob's immediate pivot to enthusiastic dancing with the man who was blocking him is perfectly in character: Gob is easily distracted by any opportunity for social performance. 'Steve Holt!' as a recurring punctuation for Gob's excitement is good running-gag material.

jokes.text: Steve Holt! — spoken as Gob spots a male student and calls his name with inexplicable enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Gob is at a high school dance looking for Shannon.
jokes.punchline: 'Steve Holt!' delivered with excited recognition — suggesting Gob knows this high school student, which at a high school dance is inherently suspicious.
jokes.explanation: 'Steve Holt!' is one of the show's most enduring running gags — the exclamation mark is in the delivery, always said with the same enthusiasm. On first watch it's mildly funny as a non sequitur; rewatchers know Steve Holt may be Gob's son, making Gob's presence at his high school both more and less suspicious. High quotability because it became a cultural reference.

jokes.text: Excuse me. Look, I blew it, okay? But I bought a yearbook ad from you. Doesn't that mean anything anymore?
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to win back Shannon, who has learned he cheated on her.
jokes.punchline: His argument for forgiveness: 'I bought a yearbook ad from you. Doesn't that mean anything anymore?' — treating a yearbook ad as a legitimate romantic gesture that should offset infidelity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Gob lines. The specificity of 'I bought a yearbook ad' as a romantic currency is inspired — it's the most nakedly transactional and least romantic gesture imaginable, deployed as genuine leverage. 'Doesn't that mean anything anymore?' with its wounded tone is perfect. Highly quotable, strong character integration, excellent economy. This is what makes Gob a great comic character.

jokes.text: Oh, that's your definition of the right thing. I thought I was gonna make a dramatic entrance and tell you that I wanted to see you.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly (whom Gob has intercepted at the dance) says 'You're gonna tell George Michael the truth?' assuming Gob means to do the right thing.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'right thing' was not confession but a romantic declaration for himself. His disappointment at her definition is entirely sincere.
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-absorption is perfectly rendered — his 'right thing' is entirely self-serving. The misdirection works because we briefly think Gob might be growing, then the reveal is better: he's exactly as he always was.

jokes.text: I slept with the ethics teacher. I'm sorry. / What are you talking about? / Lindsay told me that you liked her and I'm not proud of this, but... Mrs. Whitehead. / In fact, Gob mistook the civics teacher for the ethics teacher Michael was dating.
jokes.setup: Gob has come to confess he slept with the ethics teacher. Michael is confused.
jokes.punchline: Gob slept with Mrs. Whitehead — the civics teacher, not the ethics teacher. The narrator explains: 'Man, he has a type.' Gob aimed for revenge and hit the wrong target entirely.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding comedic construction. Gob's revenge plot has collapsed — he slept with the wrong teacher entirely. The narrator's 'Man, he has a type' is a perfect dry cap. This is the episode's funniest extended beat — the entire premise of Gob's revenge mission has been a comedy of errors where he couldn't even correctly identify his target. Deeply character-integrated: Gob's incompetence extends to his sexual revenge schemes. Structure is excellent: the misdirection of 'I slept with the ethics teacher' leading to the reveal that it was actually the civics teacher is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Gob, Mrs. Whitehead was the civics teacher. / We both had her. / Yes, we have. And now we're even.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Gob slept with the wrong teacher.
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'We both had her' — meaning as students in her class. Gob immediately takes it the other way: 'Yes, we have. And now we're even.' — interpreting 'had her' in the sexual sense and declaring mutual romantic grievance settled.
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'we both had her' — academic vs. sexual — is executed with near-perfect economy. Michael's innocent meaning ('as students in her class') and Gob's immediate reinterpretation is textbook AD wordplay. 'And now we're even' seals it as a perfect exchange. Maximum quotability. This could rival 'Snip snap' for economy of comedic payoff in a single exchange.

jokes.text: What a pig. / Tell me about it. / No, you! You're the pig!
jokes.setup: Shannon overhears Gob say something piggish and calls him a pig.
jokes.punchline: Gob responds 'Tell me about it' — agreeing with her assessment, apparently thinking she's referring to someone else. Then Shannon clarifies: 'No, you! You're the pig!' — revealing his social obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Gob's complete inability to recognize he is the subject of Shannon's complaint is good character comedy. The reversal ('No, you!') is clean. Solid mid-tier joke.

jokes.text: Listen. Shannon, please. It was a one-time thing. I'm not gonna hear from her again! / Ach! / Who is Edna W? Hello?
jokes.setup: Gob swears Nazhgalia was a one-time thing and he won't hear from her again.
jokes.punchline: Gob's phone rings immediately. The caller ID reads 'Edna W' — presumably Nazhgalia's real name — proving his declaration instantly false.
jokes.explanation: Classic situation-comedy irony — the immediate phone call proving the lie. The fact that Nazhgalia's real name appears to be 'Edna W' is an additional layer of absurdity. Callback to Gob's 'she knows it was a one-time thing, totally cool' confidence. Well-timed.

jokes.text: He said that you wanted her as a mother, and I said that was ridiculous — but then I met her and kind of fell for her and thought that she would kind of make a great mother.
jokes.setup: Michael is confessing to George Michael that he had been dating Ms. Baerly. He explains that Lindsay told him George Michael wanted her as a mother figure.
jokes.punchline: Michael's dismissal of the idea ('I said that was ridiculous') is immediately followed by his admission that he agreed with it the moment he met her ('thought she would kind of make a great mother'). He did exactly what he mocked.
jokes.explanation: Michael's admission that he called the 'mother theory' ridiculous and then acted on it anyway is a good character-comedy beat. The self-awareness gap is telling. Solid callback structure.

jokes.text: Well, you know, I'm open to it. We're open to it. / Wow! / No, no, no, no. / I'm just saying someday, you know.
jokes.setup: Michael has just said Ms. Baerly 'would kind of make a great mother.' He then says he's 'open to it.'
jokes.punchline: He inadvertently says 'we're open to it' — speaking for both himself and George Michael about the same woman, treating his son's crush as a shared romantic prospect. Ms. Baerly's 'I'm not really into kids' adds the cap.
jokes.explanation: The 'we're open to it' including George Michael in Michael's romantic prospects for the same woman is an inadvertent Freudian slip that's deeply uncomfortable and funny. Ms. Baerly's 'I'm not really into kids' as a rejection of the entire situation is a clean cap.

jokes.text: I'm not really into kids. / I'm just... I'm not trying to rush anything. / Hey! The lady said no, man.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly has said she's 'not really into kids' when Michael suggests a possible future together.
jokes.punchline: George Michael jumps in with 'Hey! The lady said no, man' — defending Ms. Baerly's rejection of his own father on her behalf, while simultaneously being rejected himself.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's 'The lady said no, man' is a wonderful bit of self-defeating chivalry — he's defending the autonomy of the woman who has just rejected him too. It's both earnest and deeply ironic. Strong economy, excellent character integration.

jokes.text: I mean, we had some laughs about some very sad things, but I don't think we have a future. / And, Jeremy, I will take that cupcake.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly is ending things with Michael at the school dance.
jokes.punchline: Mid-breakup speech, without missing a beat, she intercepts a cupcake from a passing student named Jeremy. The pivot from emotional to transactional is perfectly timed.
jokes.explanation: The cupcake interception during the breakup speech is a perfect deadpan visual gag embedded in the dialogue. 'And, Jeremy, I will take that cupcake' is a beautifully economical non-sequitur cap to an emotional moment. It also characterizes Ms. Baerly as fundamentally unflappable.

jokes.text: Hey, uh, you wanna dance? / Sure, why not? (Michael asks the student who just gave Ms. Baerly the cupcake to dance)
jokes.setup: Michael has just been dumped at the diversity dance. He's now alone.
jokes.punchline: Michael asks the random cupcake-holding student to dance. The student says 'Sure, why not?' — treating this as a normal occurrence. Michael's desperation rendered in a single impulsive ask.
jokes.explanation: The deflation of Michael's romantic evening to dancing with a random student is a good comedic cap. 'Sure, why not?' is the perfect student response — neither alarmed nor particularly pleased. Good beat.

jokes.text: I can't believe that would have worked.
jokes.setup: George Michael watches Michael successfully get a dance partner after being rejected by Ms. Baerly.
jokes.punchline: 'I can't believe that would have worked' — George Michael is astonished that simply asking someone to dance at a school dance leads to dancing, implying he had considered and rejected this approach as too unlikely.
jokes.explanation: Seven words that perfectly characterize George Michael's paralytic social anxiety. He's stunned that directly asking someone to dance — the most basic social action at a dance — actually works. The line requires zero setup once you know George Michael. Economy is maximal. Deeply quotable and character-specific to a fault. One of the episode's very best lines.

jokes.text: Michael, watching his son dance with his ex-date: 'I can't believe that would have worked.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just been rejected; his son immediately gets a dance.
jokes.punchline: 'I can't believe that would have worked' — Michael processing that the simplest possible approach ('wanna dance?') worked, while his entire elaborate relationship collapsed.
jokes.explanation: Michael's flat, honest reaction to watching his son succeed by being simple where he overcomplicated everything. The deadpan economy of 'I can't believe that would have worked' captures Michael's self-aware defeat. Good closing character beat.

jokes.text: I'll light a candle of my own. That's exactly what I'll do.
jokes.setup: Buster's response to Annyong lighting candles is to declare he'll light his own.
jokes.punchline: 'That's exactly what I'll do' — the ponderous declaration of a man who thinks lighting a candle is a meaningful act of rebellion. Buster's idea of escalation is to also light a candle.
jokes.explanation: Buster's complete ineptitude at rebellion is captured in a single line. 'That's exactly what I'll do' treats a candle as a counterstrike. Pure Buster character comedy.

tmdb_overview: Michael falls for his George Michael's ethics teacher, whom his son also has a crush on. Meanwhile, Lucille adopts a 14-year old Korean boy, and George Sr.'s "Caged Wisdom" tapes have given him a new admirer.
insight: Escalation and character comedy sustain an 84.8 score across 56 rapid-fire jokes.
